title: The 25 Best Movies of the Century: No. 20 - ‘Melancholia’
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins put Lars von Trier's Melancholia at No. 20, Amanda's pick and her favorite movie. They argue its case that the depressed person is the one seeing clearly, place it in von Trier's career and Kirsten Dunst's, and revisit the 2011 Cannes press conference that nearly buried it.

films.title: Melancholia
films.note: Amanda's pick and, she says, her favorite movie. Lars von Trier splits it in two: a lavish wedding-from-hell at an estate (Kirsten Dunst as Justine, unable to hold onto a single good moment), then the sisters waiting out a rogue planet named Melancholia on a collision course with Earth. Sean reads it as von Trier's most sensitive film because he doesn't make his lead a humiliated victim; both take the ending, shown up front in a Tristan und Isolde overture of living-painting tableaux, as his argument that the depressed person is the one seeing clearly. Now on Netflix and Tubi. Dunst won Best Actress at Cannes. Justine takes her name from the suffering heroine of Sade's 1787 novel; the cast runs deep, Charlotte Gainsbourg as the anxious sister Claire, Kiefer Sutherland as the know-it-all husband John, Alexander and Stellan Skarsgård, Charlotte Rampling and John Hurt as the poisonous parents, Udo Kier as the ruined wedding planner, and Brady Corbet in a cameo.

films.title: Breaking the Waves
films.note: Widely considered von Trier's masterpiece up to this point, and one of the films Sean recommends after. Grouped with the punishing English-language run where the heroine is a victim, the quality Melancholia breaks from.

films.title: Dancer in the Dark
films.note: Cited for the same punishing streak, and for the misconduct accusations Björk later made about the shoot, which didn't visibly dent von Trier's career.

films.title: Dogville
films.note: One of the hard-to-sit-through English-language films where you arrive at the same bleak verdict as Melancholia, but without the sensitivity.

films.title: Manderlay
films.note: Named alongside Dogville as the punishing, endurance-test phase before Melancholia softened the approach.

films.title: The Idiots
films.note: Sean's other von Trier recommendation, filed as the more fun one, for lack of a better word. A Dogme 95 film, the grainy handheld style Melancholia keeps the tools of but paints with.

films.title: Nymphomaniac
films.note: The film after Melancholia, and evidence of the turn: still unpleasant across three-plus hours, but more gorgeous to look at.

films.title: The House That Jack Built
films.note: Von Trier's 2018 serial-killer film with Matt Dillon, and his last released feature; he was diagnosed with Parkinson's in 2022.

films.title: Funny Games
films.note: Brady Corbet acted in Haneke's English-language remake; Sean uses it to place Corbet among the provocateur lineage (Haneke, Gaspar Noé, von Trier) he learned from.

films.title: Last Year at Marienbad
films.note: Named as the kind of existentialist European film Melancholia pays homage to.

films.title: Up
films.note: Amanda's comparison for the overture: the opposite of Up's first ten minutes, one showing the possibility of life and one its impossibility, both about accepting death.

segments.heading: The Kirsten Dunst filmography
segments.note: the career this did and didn't reset

films.title: Bring It On
films.note: The cute, bubbly blonde typecasting Melancholia was supposed to break; her first true adult role, per Sean.

films.title: Marie Antoinette
films.note: The Sofia Coppola film Sean calls what if a historical figure were just a teenager. Like Melancholia, it leans on a nearly wordless Dunst performance.

films.title: Bachelorette
films.note: A great, fun comedy she took right after Melancholia, cited as proof the following work wasn't bad, just curiously small.

films.title: The Two Faces of January
films.note: Part of the confusing five-year stretch after Cannes where nothing quite of note landed.

films.title: Midnight Special
films.note: A modest part, grouped with the smaller roles that followed the Best Actress win instead of the Cate Blanchett trajectory the press predicted.

films.title: Hidden Figures
films.note: Another modest part in the post-Melancholia lull.

films.title: The Beguiled
films.note: A good Coppola film, Sean's least favorite of the Sofia movies; the kind of you're-35-now part he thinks she'd earned.

films.title: Woodshock
films.note: The Mulleavy sisters' film, filed with the odd, off-the-radar choices of those years.

films.title: The Power of the Dog
films.note: The reset, per Sean, that re-established her as one of our great American actors deserving of these parts.

films.title: Civil War
films.note: Named among the four films that finally arrived, the kind Sean expected right after Melancholia.

films.title: The Entertainment System Is Down
films.note: The upcoming Ruben Östlund film, cited as her next; grouped with the belated arrival of major roles.

films.title: Roofman
films.note: Also named among the four current films marking her return to the center.

picks.title: Stalker
picks.note: Tarkovsky, a lighthouse for von Trier: science fiction smashed into the drama of despair without overexplaining.

picks.title: Solaris
picks.note: The other Tarkovsky Sean names as deeply synchronized with Melancholia.

picks.title: Breaking the Waves
picks.note: For von Trier fans, the film long considered his masterpiece before this one.

picks.title: The Idiots
picks.note: Another von Trier to think about alongside Melancholia, and the more fun of them.

picks.title: Rachel Getting Married
picks.note: One of the two wedding movies they each put down; the Jonathan Demme, about a difficult, depressed woman at a beautiful family event.

picks.title: Margot at the Wedding
picks.note: Amanda's wedding-movie pick: the Noah Baumbach, a tough sit about a woman who is not getting married and is terribly depressed.

picks.title: The Virgin Suicides
picks.note: Dunst in a different life phase working similar themes: depression, something unknowable, what she communicates while barely speaking.

picks.title: Birth
picks.note: Jonathan Glazer's film, another lost-Kidman movie; not on the list, but one Amanda would happily slot in.
